branches of learning. But for his erudition and able exposition many
passages and technical terms in this work would have remained
obscure to the readers. His quotations from many unknown works
are a boon to the world of scholars. His language is simple and
forceful. He is well informed about many technical subjects such as
1. 'India' I. 157, etc.

astronomy, mathematics, poetics, Agama or Tantra, perfumery, etc.
Hisfrankness about his lack of practical knowledge of perfumes is
praiseworthy.1 Even here his knowledge is by far above the average.
For, he quotes from Uvara's work in Prdkrla on this subject in order
to explain the terms Vedha and Bodha.
He mentions several authorities and quotes extensively
from their works : (1) Unnamed authorities; (2) Agamavidah;
(3) Acdryavi$nucandra; (4) Aryabhata; (5) Hvara; (6) Rfiputra;
